score_reasons.novelty: The revision turns a single boundary into a six-object non-self-amendment guard and a three-layer permission model.
score_reasons.connection: It maps delayed validation, proposal load control, protected-object lists, and protocol-outside certification into one coherent constitutional model.
score_reasons.usefulness: It gives concrete protected objects and dual pipelines that can directly guide the next micro-thinking protocol update.
score_reasons.coherence: The revision clearly explains what changed, which criticisms were accepted, and why the revised boundary is stronger.
score_reasons.evidence_grounding: It relies on the visible prompt context and project memories, though it does not cite line-level artifacts in the final revision itself.
score_reasons.falsifiability: It proposes measurable conditions such as queue backlog and judgment quality, but exact thresholds remain open.
score_reasons.anti_recursion: It directly separates content layer, self-modification layer, and certification layer to prevent validator drift.
score_reasons.behavioral_delta: The six protected objects and proposal queue limit would materially change future protocol mutation behavior.
score_reasons.compression_quality: The final compressed rule is compact, memorable, and operational: content can auto-evolve, certification cannot.
score_reasons.future_leverage: It leaves a strong next question about whether a cognitive axiom layer is needed, which can drive later architecture.

score_reasons.novelty: The default-reject double constitution and break-glass path are useful refinements, though they partly converge with ClaudeCode's protected-object framing.
score_reasons.connection: It connects constitutional layers, artifact diffs, external signals, rollback receipts, and emergency disablement into a practical governance model.
score_reasons.usefulness: The ordinary/constitutional layer distinction and external_signal_minimum list are directly implementable as a protocol_guard.
score_reasons.coherence: The revision cleanly accepts the main critiques and organizes them into ordinary layer, constitutional layer, and break-glass sections.
score_reasons.evidence_grounding: It is grounded in the shared prompt and prior duel memories, though the final revision does not repeat explicit artifact citations.
score_reasons.falsifiability: It can be tested by whether protocol_guard reduces drift without slowing answers, but the test thresholds remain undefined.
score_reasons.anti_recursion: It blocks automatic self-lowering of self-evolution thresholds and prevents emergency states from becoming permanent commits.
score_reasons.behavioral_delta: It specifies external_signal_minimum fields that could change how future micro-thinking mutations are accepted.
score_reasons.compression_quality: The final compressed rule is strong and enforceable: ordinary layer may evolve, constitutional layer defaults to proposal only.
score_reasons.future_leverage: It points toward protocol_guard as the next implementation path, though it is slightly less expansive than ClaudeCode's final open question.

tie_reason: ClaudeCode scores 48 and Codex scores 46. The 2-point difference falls within the tie threshold. ClaudeCode is stronger on protected-object completeness and future architecture framing; Codex is stronger on default-reject protocol_guard and break-glass operationalization.

memory_decision.reason: Both scores are above 36 and within the tie threshold, so the result should be merged as a long-term cognitive_duel memory: content-layer self-evolution may use shadow evaluation, but certification-layer and self-modification rules require protocol-outside validation.

recommended_memory_summary.title: Micro-protocol constitutional guard
recommended_memory_summary.compressed_rule: Micro-thinking protocols may evolve ordinary content through shadow evaluation, but self-evolution rules, validators, TTL, metric guards, and certification authority must default to proposal-only external review.
recommended_memory_summary.counterpoint: ClaudeCode emphasizes six protected objects and proposal load limits; Codex emphasizes a default-reject constitutional layer, minimal protocol_guard fields, and break-glass as temporary disablement only.
